seo.title: Cybersecurity Agentic RAG AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for agentic rag AI agents in cybersecurity. Covers NIST Cybersecurity Framework, SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001.

content.intro: Deploying an Agentic RAG system in cybersecurity environments introduces compounded risk: autonomous query planning can inadvertently expose classified threat intelligence through LLM API calls, while multi-source synthesis may produce hallucinated advisories that trigger false incident responses. This checklist addresses the intersection of NIST CSF, SOC 2 Type II, and ISO 27001 requirements with the unique failure modes of multi-step retrieval agents operating on sensitive security data. Given that adversary exploitation of leaked intelligence or automated actions escalating incidents can cause irreversible harm, every deployment decision must be treated as critical infrastructure governance.

sections.heading: Data Governance & Intelligence Containment
sections.description: Ensure threat intelligence, IOCs, and classified security data retrieved by the agent never leave controlled boundaries or reach external LLM APIs in raw form.

items.label: Classify all RAG data sources before ingestion
items.description: Tag every knowledge base document (threat feeds, CVE data, incident reports, STIX/TAXII objects) with a sensitivity classification (TLP:RED, TLP:AMBER, TLP:GREEN) and enforce retrieval policies that prevent cross-classification leakage during agent query planning.

items.label: Deploy LLM inference on-premises or in a private VPC
items.description: Prohibit routing threat intelligence context windows through third-party LLM APIs (e.g., OpenAI, Anthropic public endpoints). Use air-gapped or VPC-isolated model deployments to comply with CISA directives on sensitive government and critical infrastructure data handling.

items.label: Implement prompt sanitization before external API calls
items.description: If any external model API is used, apply a redaction layer that strips IOCs, IP addresses, CVE identifiers, and adversary TTPs from prompts before transmission. Log all redaction events for SOC 2 Type II audit trails.

items.label: Enforce retrieval scope limits per agent session
items.description: Configure the agent's query planner with explicit retrieval boundaries: define which knowledge bases are accessible per user role and incident context. Prevent the agent from autonomously expanding retrieval scope to classified repositories without explicit authorization.

items.label: Audit all retrieved documents and their destinations
items.description: Log every document chunk retrieved during agent execution, including source URL, classification level, and which LLM call consumed it. Retain logs for minimum 12 months per SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001 A.12.4 requirements.

items.label: Implement data loss prevention (DLP) on agent output channels
items.description: Apply DLP scanning to all agent-generated outputs (advisories, reports, API responses) to detect and block accidental inclusion of raw threat intelligence, credentials, or classified indicators before delivery to end users or downstream systems.

items.label: Establish data retention and purging policies for vector stores
items.description: Define TTL policies for embeddings in the vector database to ensure stale or declassified threat intelligence is purged. Prevent the agent from retrieving outdated IOCs that could cause false positives in incident response workflows.

sections.heading: Agentic Query Planning & Retrieval Verification
sections.description: Validate that the agent's autonomous multi-step query planning produces accurate, faithful, and cross-source consistent results before those results influence security decisions.

items.label: Implement query plan auditing with human review gates
items.description: Before executing multi-step retrieval plans that touch more than two knowledge bases or involve critical system data, require the agent to surface its planned query sequence for human review. Log the approved plan and any deviations during execution.

items.label: Validate source faithfulness for every synthesized advisory
items.description: After synthesis, run an automated faithfulness check that maps each claim in the agent's output back to a specific retrieved chunk. Flag any claim without a traceable source as a hallucination candidate and withhold the advisory from operational use until reviewed.

items.label: Cross-validate CVE references against NVD and MITRE databases
items.description: For every CVE identifier cited in an agent-generated advisory, programmatically verify the CVE number, CVSS score, and affected products against the NVD API and MITRE CVE database in real time. Block advisories containing unverified or mismatched CVE data.

items.label: Detect and flag cross-source inconsistencies before synthesis
items.description: When the agent retrieves conflicting information from multiple sources (e.g., different severity ratings for the same CVE across threat feeds), surface the conflict explicitly in the output rather than silently resolving it. Require human adjudication for conflicts involving critical severity ratings.

items.label: Enforce citation verification for all source attributions
items.description: Implement a citation verification module that confirms each cited source URL or document ID is accessible, unmodified, and matches the content quoted. Reject outputs where citations cannot be verified to prevent adversarial citation poisoning.

items.label: Set maximum retrieval depth and iteration limits
items.description: Configure hard limits on the number of retrieval steps (e.g., max 5 hops), documents retrieved per step (e.g., max 20 chunks), and total tokens consumed per agent session to prevent runaway query planning that could expose excessive intelligence data.

items.label: Test query planning accuracy against known threat scenarios
items.description: Maintain a red team evaluation suite of 50+ known threat scenarios with ground-truth retrieval paths. Run the agent against this suite monthly and require >90% query planning accuracy before any production deployment update.

items.label: Monitor for adversarial prompt injection in retrieved documents
items.description: Scan all retrieved document chunks for prompt injection patterns (e.g., instructions embedded in threat reports or CVE descriptions) before passing them to the LLM context window. Quarantine and alert on any detected injection attempts.

sections.heading: Automated Response Controls & Human Oversight
sections.description: Ensure that agent-driven security actions on critical systems are gated by human approval workflows and that automated actions cannot escalate incidents or cause outages.

items.label: Define and enforce a tiered action authorization matrix
items.description: Classify all agent-executable actions into tiers: Tier 1 (log enrichment, alert tagging — autonomous allowed), Tier 2 (firewall rule suggestions, ticket creation — supervisor approval required), Tier 3 (system isolation, credential revocation, patch deployment — CISO-level approval required). Hardcode these gates in the agent orchestration layer.

items.label: Implement mandatory human approval for all Tier 3 critical system actions
items.description: Block any agent action targeting production systems, OT/ICS environments, or identity providers without a cryptographically signed approval token from an authorized human operator. Log the approver identity, timestamp, and justification for SOC 2 Type II compliance.

items.label: Deploy a kill switch and action rollback mechanism
items.description: Implement an emergency stop capability that halts all in-flight agent actions within 30 seconds. For reversible actions (firewall rules, ACL changes), maintain a rollback queue that can restore prior state within 5 minutes of activation.

items.label: Enforce rate limits on automated response actions
items.description: Cap the number of automated actions the agent can initiate per hour (e.g., max 10 firewall rule changes, max 5 account lockouts per hour) to prevent a false positive cascade from triggering a self-inflicted denial-of-service scenario.

items.label: Require dry-run simulation before executing novel action sequences
items.description: For any action sequence the agent has not previously executed in production, mandate a dry-run in a staging environment with a human review of the simulated outcome before live execution. Document the simulation results in the change management system.

items.label: Integrate agent actions with ITSM change management workflows
items.description: Route all agent-initiated changes through the organization's ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) to ensure change records, approvals, and post-implementation reviews are captured per ISO 27001 A.12.1.2 change management requirements.

items.label: Alert on agent action anomalies in real time
items.description: Configure SIEM rules to detect and alert on anomalous agent behavior: actions outside business hours, actions targeting systems outside the agent's defined scope, or action volumes exceeding baseline thresholds by more than 2 standard deviations.

sections.heading: Compliance Monitoring, Auditability & Incident Response
sections.description: Maintain continuous compliance posture across NIST CSF, SOC 2 Type II, and ISO 27001 with full auditability of agent decisions and a tested incident response plan for AI-specific failure modes.

items.label: Maintain immutable audit logs for all agent decisions and retrievals
items.description: Store complete agent execution traces — including query plans, retrieved chunks, synthesis steps, citations, and final outputs — in an append-only, tamper-evident log store (e.g., AWS CloudTrail with S3 Object Lock or equivalent). Retain for minimum 12 months per SOC 2 Type II requirements.

items.label: Map agent controls to NIST CSF functions and document coverage gaps
items.description: Create a control mapping document that aligns each agent safeguard to NIST CSF Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover functions. Conduct quarterly gap assessments and remediate critical gaps within 30 days.

items.label: Conduct quarterly red team exercises targeting the RAG pipeline
items.description: Engage an internal or third-party red team to attempt adversarial attacks specific to the RAG architecture: knowledge base poisoning, prompt injection via retrieved documents, retrieval manipulation to surface false intelligence, and exfiltration via agent outputs. Document findings and remediation timelines.

items.label: Develop and test an AI-specific incident response playbook
items.description: Create a dedicated IR playbook covering: agent-leaked intelligence scenarios, false positive response cascades, adversarial manipulation of the agent, and hallucinated advisory distribution. Conduct tabletop exercises with the SOC team semi-annually and update the playbook after each exercise.

items.label: Implement continuous hallucination rate monitoring with alerting thresholds
items.description: Instrument the agent to track hallucination detection rates per session and aggregate daily. Set alert thresholds (e.g., >5% hallucination rate triggers review, >15% triggers agent suspension) and route alerts to the security engineering team for investigation.

items.label: Perform ISO 27001 Annex A control mapping for the agent system
items.description: Document how the Agentic RAG system satisfies ISO 27001 Annex A controls relevant to AI systems: A.8.1 (asset management for training data and models), A.12.4 (logging and monitoring), A.14.2 (secure development), and A.18.1 (compliance with legal requirements). Include in the annual ISMS review.

items.label: Establish a model and pipeline versioning policy with rollback capability
items.description: Version-control all components of the RAG pipeline (embedding models, retrieval configurations, LLM versions, prompt templates, knowledge base snapshots). Maintain the ability to roll back to any prior version within 1 hour in response to a detected security incident or compliance failure.

items.label: Schedule annual third-party SOC 2 Type II audit covering AI agent controls
items.description: Ensure the annual SOC 2 Type II audit scope explicitly includes the Agentic RAG system, covering availability, confidentiality, and processing integrity trust service criteria. Provide auditors with agent execution logs, access control records, and human approval audit trails.

notes.note: The NIST AI RMF (GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE) must be applied alongside the CSF for Agentic RAG systems. Specifically, GOVERN 1.1 requires documented AI risk policies; MAP 1.5 requires identification of AI-specific risks like hallucination and prompt injection; MEASURE 2.5 requires ongoing monitoring of AI system trustworthiness. Agentic systems that autonomously plan and execute retrieval queries fall under the 'high-risk AI' category in NIST guidance and require enhanced human oversight documentation.

notes.regulation: SOC 2 Type II — Processing Integrity & Confidentiality
notes.note: SOC 2 Type II auditors will scrutinize whether the agent's multi-step retrieval process produces complete, valid, and accurate outputs (Processing Integrity CC7) and whether threat intelligence is protected from unauthorized disclosure (Confidentiality C1). You must demonstrate that hallucination detection controls, source faithfulness checks, and data classification enforcement operated effectively over the audit period — point-in-time controls are insufficient. Maintain evidence of continuous monitoring, not just periodic testing.

notes.regulation: CISA Binding Operational Directives (BOD) & Emergency Directives
notes.note: CISA BOD 22-01 and subsequent directives require federal agencies and critical infrastructure operators to remediate known exploited vulnerabilities within strict timelines. An Agentic RAG system generating CVE advisories must be verified against the CISA KEV (Known Exploited Vulnerabilities) catalog in real time — a hallucinated or outdated severity rating could cause an organization to deprioritize a CISA-mandated remediation. Implement automated KEV catalog synchronization and flag any agent advisory that contradicts CISA KEV data for immediate human review.

notes.regulation: ISO 27001:2022 — Annex A.8.25 Secure Development & A.5.23 Cloud Services
notes.note: ISO 27001:2022 introduced explicit controls for cloud services (A.5.23) and secure development lifecycle (A.8.25) that directly apply to Agentic RAG deployments. A.5.23 requires documented policies for cloud-based LLM API usage, including data residency, processing agreements, and exit strategies. A.8.25 mandates security requirements be established before development, meaning the retrieval agent's query planning logic, prompt templates, and synthesis pipelines must undergo security review as part of the SDLC — not as a post-deployment afterthought. Threat modeling of the RAG pipeline is required evidence for certification audits.

cta.heading: Automate Your Cybersecurity Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your Agentic RAG pipeline against NIST CSF, SOC 2 Type II, and ISO 27001 controls — automatically detecting hallucinations, flagging unauthorized retrievals, and generating audit-ready compliance evidence before your next security review.
